findings.description: TDD Anchor `test_get_list_excludes_email`: the test correctly verifies that `data.submissions[0]` does not have an `email` property. However, it relies on the mock returning an object without `email` — it does not assert that the Prisma `select` call explicitly excludes `email`. A stronger assertion would also check that `mockFindMany` was called with a `select` object that does NOT contain `email: true`.
findings.suggestion: Add: `expect(mockFindMany).toHaveBeenCalledWith(expect.objectContaining({ select: expect.not.objectContaining({ email: true }) }))` — though this is enhancement-level, not a blocker.

findings.description: TDD Anchor `test_get_detail_includes_orphan_responses`: the test verifies that `data.responses` has length 2 and that the orphan (fieldId=null) is present. This is a solid test. One minor gap: it does not verify that filtering is NOT applied — i.e., a naive implementation that only returns responses where `fieldId != null` would fail this test correctly, which is the right behavior.
findings.suggestion: No change needed. The existing assertion is sufficient to catch the common mistake of filtering out null fieldIds.

findings.description: TDD Anchor `test_patch_status_unknown_value`: the test mocks `findUnique` to return a submission even for an unknown status value. However, the implementation correctly returns 400 BEFORE reaching `findUnique` (the enum validation happens first on line 31-34 of the route). This means `mockFindUnique` is set up but never called in this test path. The test passes correctly, but the setup is misleading about code execution order.
findings.suggestion: Remove the `mockFindUnique.mockResolvedValue(...)` line from `test_patch_status_unknown_value` to accurately reflect that `findUnique` is never called for unknown status values. Also consider adding: `expect(mockFindUnique).not.toHaveBeenCalled()` as a positive assertion.

findings.description: Coverage of `IN_REVIEW→REJECTED` and `IN_REVIEW→APPROVED` valid transitions is present (lines 97-130), as is coverage of `APPROVED→*` terminal state (lines 167-180) and self-transition `IN_REVIEW→IN_REVIEW` (lines 182-195). The test suite covers more than the minimum TDD anchors, which is positive.
findings.suggestion: No action needed. Additional coverage of edge cases beyond anchors is good practice.

findings.description: The mock for `@/lib/db` declares `findUnique` and `update` on the `formSubmission` object even though `route.ts` only uses `findMany`. This is not harmful but adds unnecessary noise to the mock setup.
findings.suggestion: Trim the mock to only declare `findMany` for the list route test file. This makes the mock intent clearer.

summary: All 10 TDD Anchor tests from the task file are covered and passing. The test suite runs 21 tests across 3 files with 100% pass rate. Mocking strategy is correct: `next-auth`, `@/lib/auth/auth-options`, and `@/lib/db` are all properly mocked before dynamic imports, avoiding module caching issues. Minor observations: one test (`test_patch_status_unknown_value`) sets up a `findUnique` mock that is never called due to early-return validation, and the list route mock includes unused methods. These are cosmetic issues that do not affect test correctness. The assertion quality is good — tests check both HTTP status codes and response body content. No missing coverage for required transitions or auth scenarios.
